title: Git Foundations
description: A beginner Git course with three standard modules and one final capstone. Learners work locally first, moving from an ordinary folder to a readable repository history without introducing branches, remotes, GitHub, or advanced history rewriting.

modules.title: Create Your First Repository
modules.description: Start with an ordinary folder, initialize Git on main, read what Git sees, and record the project’s first meaningful snapshot.
modules.purpose: Build the repository model in the order a beginner actually uses it: initialize, configure, inspect, stage, commit, and verify.
learningObjectives: Explain why one project with history is safer than a stack of duplicate final folders.
Initialize an ordinary folder as a Git repository on main.
Configure a repository-only author identity and read untracked, staged, committed, and clean state.
Stage a deliberate first snapshot, commit it, and verify the resulting history.
guidedExercises: Turn a prepared ordinary folder into a repository with git init -b main.
Read repository state before and after staging without memorizing decorative output.
Carry one small project from untracked files to a clean first commit.
quizFocus: working tree and staging area state
the transition from staged files to a committed snapshot
modules.moduleProject: Build a Field Notes repository in five cumulative milestones, ending with one clear initial commit and a clean working tree.

sections.title: From Folder to First Commit
sections.summary: Understand the problem Git solves, initialize your first repository, read its state, and move one deliberate change into history.

topics.title: Why Version Control Matters
topics.summary: Open with a standalone story about a project losing its history, then distinguish Git from duplicate final folders and explain the safe local course workspace.
learningGoals: Explain the problem Git solves for a changing project.
Distinguish Git as a local version-control system from GitHub as a hosted collaboration platform.
Describe the inspect, change, verify habit used throughout the course.

topics.title: Initialize Your First Repository
topics.summary: Begin with an ordinary project folder, create its Git metadata on main, inspect HEAD, and set a repository-only author identity.
learningGoals: Initialize an existing folder with main as the starting branch.
Explain what the .git directory and HEAD represent without editing repository metadata directly.
Configure user.name and user.email for one repository only.

topics.title: Read the Repository State
topics.summary: Use full and short status views to distinguish new files, tracked changes, staged changes, and a clean working tree.
learningGoals: Interpret the important sections of git status.
Use git status --short to read compact file state.
Distinguish untracked, modified, staged, and clean states.

topics.title: Move a Change into History
topics.summary: Follow one file from an ordinary edit through the staging area into a committed snapshot, checking the repository at each boundary.
learningGoals: Describe the working tree, staging area, and commit history in plain language.
Stage one intended change and verify exactly what the next commit will contain.
Create a meaningful commit and confirm the working tree is clean.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.summary: Build the Field Notes repository from an ordinary folder to a clean first commit, one milestone at a time.

topics.title: Project: Build the Field Notes Repository
topics.summary: Guide a neighborhood garden’s field notes through initialization, local identity, status, staging, and the first trustworthy snapshot.
learningGoals: Initialize a real project folder on main before using later repository commands.
Configure a repository-only identity and distinguish untracked from staged files.
Create one meaningful initial commit and verify a clean working tree.

projectBrief.scenario: A neighborhood garden coordinator keeps plot observations in a folder, but every revision currently replaces the previous one. The next volunteer needs a trustworthy starting point before more notes are added.
projectBrief.role: Junior developer preparing the garden field-notes repository
projectBrief.workspace: A cumulative garden-notes/ project whose completed milestone is restored automatically before the next step opens.
projectBrief.deliverable: A five-milestone local repository on main with a repository-only identity, three tracked project files, one meaningful initial commit, and a clean working tree.
requirements: The learner’s first Git command is git init -b main; platform fixture preparation must remain invisible.
Each step begins from the complete state produced by the previous milestone.
Use only repository-local configuration for user.name and user.email.
Grade repository state rather than exact hashes, timestamps, absolute paths, or decorative output.
Every learner-facing prompt names only the Git and prerequisite navigation commands the learner should type, and explicitly says to press Enter.
Never expose .zoeskoul/setup.sh in prompts, hints, starterCode, solutionCode, or terminal expectations.

stepLadder.title: Initialize the Field Notes Repository
stepLadder.requirement: Enter garden-notes/, initialize the ordinary folder on main, and verify the current branch.

stepLadder.title: Set the Repository Identity
stepLadder.requirement: Set the repository-only learner name and email, then verify both local values.

stepLadder.title: Read the Untracked Project Files
stepLadder.requirement: Use short status to identify README.md and the two observation files before anything is staged.

stepLadder.title: Stage the Field Notes
stepLadder.requirement: Stage only README.md and observations/, then inspect the staged file names.

stepLadder.title: Create the First Snapshot
stepLadder.requirement: Commit the staged files as Start garden field notes, inspect the one-line history, and verify a clean working tree.

modules.title: Build Meaningful History
modules.description: Turn related edits into focused commits that another developer can read and inspect.
modules.purpose: Inspect differences, stage intentionally, write meaningful commit messages, and read the resulting history.
learningObjectives: Use git diff to inspect unstaged changes.
Use git add and git diff --staged to prepare one focused snapshot.
Create cohesive commits with meaningful imperative messages.
Read history with git log --oneline and inspect a commit with git show.
guidedExercises: Separate two unrelated edits before staging.
Build and inspect a readable multi-commit history.
modules.moduleProject: Build a two-change Community Website history with focused commits and a clean final state.

sections.title: From Edits to Understandable Snapshots
sections.summary: Follow a small change from diff to staging to commit, then read the history it creates.

topics.title: Inspect Unstaged Changes with git diff
topics.summary: Read line-level differences before deciding what belongs in the next commit.
learningGoals: Use git diff to inspect tracked unstaged edits.
Read added and removed lines without treating the diff header as file content.
Notice unrelated edits before they are accidentally grouped together.

topics.title: Stage Intentionally
topics.summary: Use git add to prepare selected files and git diff --staged to verify the exact snapshot before committing.
learningGoals: Stage one file without automatically staging every workspace change.
Use git diff --staged to inspect the proposed commit.
Move a file between unstaged and staged state without losing its content.

topics.title: Create Focused Commits
topics.summary: Record one coherent change with a message that explains the purpose of the snapshot.
learningGoals: Create a commit from staged changes.
Write a concise imperative commit subject.
Explain why commit boundaries should match one meaningful purpose.

topics.title: Read History with log and show
topics.summary: Use compact history and commit inspection to understand what changed without opening every file manually.
learningGoals: Read commit order with git log --oneline.
Inspect one commit with git show.
Relate the current HEAD snapshot to earlier commits.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.summary: Build a two-change Community Website history with focused commits and a clean final state.

topics.title: Project: Build the Community Website History
topics.summary: Create a readable sequence of cohesive commits for a small community website instead of saving unrelated changes in one snapshot.
learningGoals: Use diff views to separate unrelated edits before staging.
Create focused commits with meaningful messages.
Read the finished history and explain what changed in each snapshot.

projectBrief.scenario: A community center website has a plain home page and an events page. Staff want the next updates recorded as a history that another developer can understand without opening every file.
projectBrief.role: Junior web maintainer organizing a readable local history
projectBrief.workspace: One cumulative terminal workspace rooted at community-site/ with index.html, events.html, styles.css, and an initialized repository
projectBrief.deliverable: A six-step history with separate navigation and events commits, exact authored messages, preserved file content, and a clean final working tree.
requirements: Keep each commit focused on one visible purpose.
Inspect unstaged and staged differences before every commit.
Use the previous complete repository state as the next step starter.
Grade commit count, commit messages, HEAD file content, and clean state with gitExpectations.
Do not require an exact git log hash or timestamp.

stepLadder.title: Inspect the First Website Change
stepLadder.requirement: Read git status and git diff to identify the provided navigation edit without staging it.

stepLadder.title: Stage Only the Navigation Update
stepLadder.requirement: Stage index.html, inspect git diff --staged, and leave the unrelated events draft unstaged.

stepLadder.title: Commit the Navigation Update
stepLadder.requirement: Create the commit Add primary navigation and verify that events.html still has an unstaged change.

stepLadder.title: Prepare the Events Update
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the requested events copy, inspect its unstaged diff, and stage only events.html.

stepLadder.title: Commit the Events Update
stepLadder.requirement: Create the commit Publish weekend events while preserving the earlier navigation commit.

stepLadder.title: Read and Verify the Finished History
stepLadder.requirement: Use git log --oneline and git show to inspect the two new commits, then finish with the authored files and a clean working tree.

modules.title: Manage Tracked Files Safely
modules.description: Keep a repository clean as files are renamed, removed, ignored, or restored.
modules.purpose: Use Git-aware file operations, ignore generated artifacts, and recover from staged or unstaged mistakes without rewriting history.
learningObjectives: Read how tracked file modifications appear before and after staging.
Rename and remove tracked files with git mv and git rm.
Create and verify .gitignore rules for generated files.
Restore staged and unstaged mistakes safely.
guidedExercises: Trace a tracked file through modification and staging.
Clean a release tree with Git-aware file operations.
Recover an accidental edit while preserving intended work.
modules.moduleProject: Prepare a clean Volunteer Handbook release snapshot with ignored previews and a focused cleanup commit.

sections.title: A Repository Changes Shape
sections.summary: Manage a project whose files are changing names and purposes without losing the history that explains them.

topics.title: Modify and Verify Tracked Files
topics.summary: Follow an already tracked file through editing, diff inspection, staging, and status verification.
learningGoals: Recognize modified tracked files in full and short status.
Inspect content changes before staging them.
Verify which version is in the working tree and which version is staged.

topics.title: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
topics.summary: Use git mv and git rm so a project cleanup is immediately represented in the staging area.
learningGoals: Rename a tracked file with git mv.
Remove an obsolete tracked file with git rm.
Verify staged rename and deletion state before committing.

topics.title: Ignore Generated Files
topics.summary: Write focused .gitignore rules and verify that generated artifacts remain outside the repository history.
learningGoals: Create an exact .gitignore rule for a generated file or folder.
Use git check-ignore to verify why a path is ignored.
Explain why .gitignore does not automatically untrack a file already committed.

topics.title: Restore without Rewriting History
topics.summary: Undo an unstaged edit or unstage a file while keeping the committed history intact.
learningGoals: Use git restore to return one working-tree file to the staged or committed version.
Use git restore --staged to unstage a file while keeping its content.
Choose the narrowest restore operation for the mistake described.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.summary: Prepare a clean Volunteer Handbook release snapshot with ignored previews and a focused cleanup commit.

topics.title: Project: Prepare a Clean Release Snapshot
topics.summary: Clean a release folder by renaming tracked work, removing an obsolete file, ignoring generated previews, and restoring accidental edits safely.
learningGoals: Use Git-aware rename and removal commands for tracked files.
Create an effective .gitignore rule and verify ignored artifacts.
Restore staged and unstaged mistakes without rewriting commit history.

projectBrief.scenario: A volunteer handbook is ready for release, but the repository still contains a draft filename, an obsolete checklist, a generated preview log, and one accidental edit to the approved introduction.
projectBrief.role: Release assistant preparing a clean handbook snapshot
projectBrief.workspace: One cumulative terminal workspace rooted at volunteer-handbook/ with tracked docs/, generated preview output, and an existing baseline history
projectBrief.deliverable: A six-step cleanup ending with the correct tracked files, ignored generated output, restored approved content, one focused cleanup commit, and a clean working tree.
requirements: Use git mv and git rm for the authored tracked rename and deletion.
Keep preview.log present but ignored and untracked.
Use git restore and git restore --staged only for the authored recoverable mistakes.
Preserve the baseline commit and add exactly one cleanup commit.
Grade final files, ignored files, forbidden tracked files, commit message, and clean state with gitExpectations.

stepLadder.title: Audit the Release Workspace
stepLadder.requirement: Inspect status, tracked files, and the existing diff before changing the handbook repository.

stepLadder.title: Rename the Approved Guide
stepLadder.requirement: Rename docs/guide-draft.md to docs/volunteer-guide.md with git mv and verify the staged rename.

stepLadder.title: Remove the Obsolete Checklist
stepLadder.requirement: Remove docs/old-checklist.md with git rm while preserving the approved guide and README.

stepLadder.title: Ignore Generated Preview Output
stepLadder.requirement: Add preview.log to .gitignore, verify it with git check-ignore, and ensure preview.log is not tracked.

stepLadder.title: Restore the Accidental Introduction Edit
stepLadder.requirement: Unstage and restore the authored accidental README.md edit without discarding the intended rename, deletion, or .gitignore work.

stepLadder.title: Commit and Verify the Release Cleanup
stepLadder.requirement: Commit with Prepare handbook release and finish with the required files, ignored preview.log, two total commits, and a clean working tree.

modules.title: Final Capstone
modules.description: Build and hand off one complete local history for a neighborhood resource guide.
modules.purpose: Combine initialization, status, diff, staging, commits, history inspection, ignore rules, Git-aware file operations, and safe restore habits.
learningObjectives: Create a clean repository from an ordinary project folder.
Build four purposeful commits whose boundaries match the requested work.
Keep generated output ignored and obsolete files out of HEAD.
Verify the final history and clean working state before handoff.
guidedExercises: Inspect the complete starter workspace before initialization.
Build the final repository in six exact cumulative steps.
Validate files, commit messages, ignored paths, forbidden tracked paths, commit count, and clean state.
modules.moduleProject: Build the Neighborhood Resource Guide history through six cumulative milestones.

sections.title: Final Capstone
sections.summary: Build and hand off one complete local history for a neighborhood resource guide.

topics.title: Final Capstone: Neighborhood Resource Guide History
topics.summary: Build a complete local history for a neighborhood resource guide while keeping changes focused, generated artifacts ignored, and the final repository easy to hand off.
learningGoals: Initialize and grow one repository through a sequence of purposeful commits.
Separate content changes from release cleanup work.
Use status, diff, staging, restore, ignore, rename, removal, log, and show together.

projectBrief.scenario: A neighborhood help desk is replacing a folder of loose text files with a maintained resource guide. Future volunteers must be able to see when service information changed, recover from small mistakes, and trust the final handoff.
projectBrief.role: Junior developer preparing the neighborhood resource guide for long-term maintenance
projectBrief.workspace: One cumulative terminal workspace rooted at resource-guide/ with pages/, notes/, generated/, README.md, and no starting repository
projectBrief.deliverable: One six-step local Git capstone with four meaningful commits, ignored generated previews, a tracked file rename, a safe obsolete-file removal, required final content, and a clean handoff state.
requirements: Keep the capstone in exactly one capstone section and one capstone topic.
Carry the complete repository and workspace forward through all six steps.
Use only concepts taught in Git Foundations; do not use branches, merges, remotes, stash, revert, reset, rebase, or force operations.
Use local identity configuration and main as the initial branch.
The final history must contain exactly four commits with the authored commit messages.
Grade repository state with gitExpectations and never grade exact hashes or timestamps.

stepLadder.title: Create the Guide Baseline
stepLadder.requirement: Inspect resource-guide/, configure the local identity, initialize main, stage the initial README and pages, and commit Start neighborhood resource guide.

stepLadder.title: Add the Services Page
stepLadder.requirement: Create and validate pages/services.md, stage only that focused change, and commit Add local services page.

stepLadder.title: Publish Updated Help Desk Hours
stepLadder.requirement: Update pages/contact.md with the authored hours, inspect the diff, and commit Update help desk hours.

stepLadder.title: Ignore Generated Preview Files
stepLadder.requirement: Create the authored .gitignore rules for generated/ previews, verify the preview file is ignored, and keep the repository changes staged for the release cleanup commit.

stepLadder.title: Rename the Guide and Remove the Obsolete Note
stepLadder.requirement: Use git mv to rename pages/guide-draft.md to pages/getting-help.md, use git rm to remove notes/old-phone-tree.txt, and preserve all earlier content.

stepLadder.title: Commit and Verify the Final Handoff
stepLadder.requirement: Commit Prepare resource guide handoff, then verify the four-commit history, required HEAD files, ignored preview artifact, forbidden obsolete paths, and a clean working tree.
